children.text: CBF 01 - Introduction to Banking & Finance Environment (IBFE)
Banking is defined as the business activity of accepting deposits from the public that are repayable on demand (or after a fixed period) and using those funds for lending or investment
Financial Intermediation is the core process of acting as a middleman between surplus units (savers) and deficit units (borrowers).

In an economy without banks, a person with excess money would have to manually locate a business owner in need of a loan, assess the credit risk of that company, draft a complicated contract, and hope they are reimbursed. By combining and effectively allocating the funds of thousands of independent depositors, banks remove this friction.
Information Asymmetry: Managing unequal knowledge between borrowers and lenders
Banks possess the specialized tools, data, and legal frameworks to screen, evaluate, and monitor a borrower's true financial standing far better than any single individual could.
Maturity Transformation: Converting short-term deposits into long-term loans

Savers want their money available instantly (short-term), while borrowers need money for long periods, like a 20-year mortgage (long-term). Banks balance this pool so both sides get the timeframe they need
Risk Diversification: Spreading investments to minimize single-failure impacts
Instead of a saver lending to one business that might fail, the bank spreads the saver's money across thousands of different loans, drastically lowering the risk of capital loss
Liquidity Provision: Giving savers quick cash access while funding long-term projects

Liquidity provision ensures savers can instantly access their cash while banks fund long-term projects. Financial institutions bridge this gap by maintaining cash reserves and converting illiquid assets into ready money.
mechanism to expand the money supply through the process of lending

According to the Fractional Reserve Model Bank does more than only hold the cash. When a bank receives a $10,000 deposit and a 10% reserve requirement is applied, it keeps $1,000 in its vault and lends out the remaining $9,000. When that $9,000 is spent, it inevitably ends up deposited into another bank account somewhere else in the financial system.
Mobilization of Savings is the operational process of gathering idle cash from fragmented sources across an economy and consolidating it into a highly productive capital pool.
Facilitation of Payments is the utility service a bank provides by establishing the infrastructure needed to clear and settle financial transactions safely and rapidly.

3.1 Overview of the Sri Lankan Banking Structure
The framework of institutions, regulators, and financial intermediaries that facilitate financial intermediation, payments, savings mobilization, lending, and economic development in Sri Lanka.
The apex monetary authority responsible for regulating and supervising the financial system.
Institutions authorized to conduct full-service banking operations.
Institutions established for specialized banking functions.

The monetary authority and financial system regulator of Sri Lanka.
Established to maintain monetary stability, financial system stability, and support economic growth.
Maintain price stability and support sustainable economic growth.
